name: Aktionsart: Achievement
intuition: A dynamic change-of-state event conceived as punctual or near-instantaneous. It represents the boundary point of transition itself rather than an unfolding process phase. The event is the crossing of a definable threshold.
set_to_1_if: The target verb denotes a punctual or near-instantaneous transition into a new state (e.g., 'die', 'find', 'lose', 'reach', 'arrive', 'start', 'stop').
The event can naturally be modified by a specific time-point adverbial (e.g., 'at noon', 'at that moment'), indicating occurrence at a defined temporal boundary (e.g., 'He arrived at noon').
The event does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase; it either occurs or does not occur (e.g., one cannot meaningfully 'stop arriving').
The target verb denotes a punctual perceptual or cognitive transition into awareness or recognition (e.g., 'hear the news', 'see the truth', 'realize', 'recognize').
The event lacks measurable internal duration and is construed as occurring at a temporal boundary.
For cognitive or perceptual verbs that allow both punctual and durative readings, classify as achievement when the event denotes a single transition into awareness rather than a durative process.
set_to_0_if: The event unfolds over a measurable duration rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary.
The event is punctual but does not entail a transition into a new result state (e.g., a single knock or cough).
The event can naturally occur as the complement of 'finish', indicating completion of a durative process (e.g., 'He finished building the house').
The apparent punctual transition represents the culmination of a durative process encoded in the predicate, rather than the event itself.
exceptions: Even if an event requires extended preparation or causally preceding activity, it is classified as an achievement if the event itself is construed as a punctual boundary transition.
examples_positive: She has found the key. (Instantaneous discovery regardless of 'looking' preparatory phase -> 1)
They have reached the summit. (Instantaneous transition regardless of 'climbing' process phase ->1)
We arrived at noon. (Specific time point adverbial -> 1)
They have heard the news. (Punctual perceptual transition -> 1)
examples_negative: We have built a house. (Inherent process phase -> 0)
They have run every morning. (Durative activity without defined endpoint or goal -> 0)
He coughed once. (Punctual event that does not entail a transition into a new result state -> 0)
He coughed for five minutes. (Series of punctual events that do not transition into a new result state -> 0)
She finished reading the book. (Indicates durative process phase -> 0)
